description: OMOP CDM domain classification of the clinical concept. Determines which downstream tables and analyses apply: a Condition concept lands in OMOP CONDITION_OCCURRENCE, a Measurement concept in MEASUREMENT, a Drug in DRUG_EXPOSURE, etc. RoP follows OMOP's domain enumeration as the controlling vocabulary for clinical concept categorization.

value_definitions.Condition: Diagnoses, signs, symptoms, clinical findings (SNOMED, ICD-10/11, ICD-9 source vocabs)
value_definitions.Drug: Drug exposures, formulations, ingredients (RxNorm, ATC, DM+D source vocabs)
value_definitions.Procedure: Procedures performed (SNOMED-Procedure, CPT, HCPCS, ICD-10-PCS source vocabs)
value_definitions.Measurement: Lab tests, vital signs, structured measurements (LOINC primarily, SNOMED-Measurement secondarily)
value_definitions.Observation: Clinical observations not fitting other domains (SNOMED-Observable, structured questionnaire items)
value_definitions.Device: Medical devices used (SNOMED-Device, GMDN, GUDID source vocabs)
value_definitions.Specimen: Biological specimens (SNOMED-Specimen — distinct from Theme 6 biosample which is RoP-internal lineage)
value_definitions.Death: Death events and causes (ICD-10 underlying cause primarily)
value_definitions.Episode: Care episodes spanning multiple visits (RoP-derived primarily)
value_definitions.Other: Extension category for concepts not yet domain-classified

metadata_.rationale: Adopting OMOP's domain classification as the controlling vocabulary aligns RoP's clinical concept layer with the largest established harmonization standard. ~200M-row clinical datasets in the OHDSI network already use this classification.

description: OMOP concept_id — the canonical pivot identifier across vocabularies. Two clinical concept rows referencing the same standard_code are semantically equivalent regardless of source vocabulary. Numeric integer; 0 indicates unmapped.

metadata_.rationale: OMOP concept_id is the only cross-vocabulary identifier that scales across all clinical domains. Without it, every analysis tool reinvents cross-vocab mapping from scratch.

description: Preferred concept name in the standard vocabulary (OMOP concept.concept_name). Examples: 'Type 2 diabetes mellitus', 'Metformin 500 MG Oral Tablet', 'Glucose [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma', 'Echocardiogram'.

Standard vocabulary that OMOP designates as authoritative for this concept's domain. SNOMED for most Conditions, RxNorm for Drugs, LOINC for Measurements, SNOMED+CPT for Procedures, MedDRA for adverse events. The same concept may have a non-standard representation in many source vocabularies but a single canonical standard.

Source-vocabulary code as captured at the data origin. Examples: 'E11.9' (ICD-10-CM for type 2 diabetes), '250.00' (ICD-9-CM for the same), '2345-7' (LOINC for serum glucose), '6809' (RxNorm ingredient code for metformin), 'I26.9' (ICD-10 for unspecified pulmonary embolism). The source code is what was actually recorded; the standard code is OMOP's harmonized form.

Source vocabulary the source code originated from. Same enum as ClinicalConceptStandardVocabulary plus historical/regional vocabularies (ICD-9, ICD-9-CM, KCD-7, JMDC, etc.) that aren't standard but are commonly seen in source data.

Release version of the source vocabulary. Critical because coding conventions change across releases (ICD-10-CM 2023 vs 2024, RxNorm monthly releases, LOINC 2.78). A concept's interpretation can shift across versions even when the code string is identical.

Cross-vocabulary mappings for this concept. JSON array of {vocabulary, code, version, mapping_type} entries. Provides the cross-vocab lookup graph: a concept that is SNOMED:44054006 in OMOP-standard form might map to ICD-10:E11.9, ICD-9-CM:250.00, MeSH:D003924, KCD-7:E11. Precomputed at bundle build time from OMOP CONCEPT_RELATIONSHIP.

schema_per_entry.mapping_type: Maps to | Maps to value | Source-derived | RxNorm has ing | etc. (OMOP relationship_id)

description: Concept ancestors in the OMOP CONCEPT_ANCESTOR table. JSON array of {ancestor_concept_id, min_separation, max_separation} entries. Enables 'rolled-up' analyses: querying for 'Type 2 diabetes' should also surface concepts descending from the parent 'Diabetes mellitus'. Critical for cohort-definition reproducibility — without explicit hierarchy, two analysts asking 'how many T2D cases' get different answers.

OMOP-style validity window. JSON object {valid_start_date, valid_end_date, invalid_reason}. Concepts retire over time as vocabularies update; an analysis using a retired code should know it's retired. Default valid_end_date = '2099-12-31' (still valid).

Semantic tags inherited from the source vocabulary. SNOMED uses semantic tags like 'disorder', 'finding', 'procedure', 'body structure', 'substance', 'organism'. These guide downstream interpretation: a 'finding' is observable but doesn't necessarily indicate disease, while a 'disorder' implies pathology. JSON array.

Value sets this concept belongs to. JSON array of {value_set_id, value_set_name, value_set_authority} entries. Enables phenotype-definition reuse: a 'type 2 diabetes broad' value set bundles dozens of related concepts; cohort definitions reference the value set rather than enumerating concepts. Examples: PheKB phenotypes, OHDSI Phoebe phenotypes, ATLAS-derived cohort definitions.

For Measurement-domain concepts, the OMOP concept_id of the canonical unit of measure. Example: serum glucose (LOINC 2345-7, OMOP 3004501) has canonical unit mg/dL (UCUM 'mg/dL', OMOP 8840). The unit is part of the concept's identity for measurements — 'glucose' alone is ambiguous between mg/dL and mmol/L. Null for non-Measurement domains.

metadata_.format: OMOP concept_id pointing into UCUM-domain concepts
metadata_.rationale: Without unit-as-concept, mg/dL vs mmol/L vs mg/L ambiguity becomes silent error in cross-cohort meta-analysis. Resolving the unit to a concept_id makes the unit explicit and machine-checkable.
